questions.questionText: The quality (penetrating power) of an X-ray beam is primarily controlled by:
questions.optionB: kVp (kilovoltage peak — determines maximum energy of X-ray photons, hence penetrating ability)
questions.explanation: Core concept: kVp controls QUALITY (energy/penetration); mA controls QUANTITY (number of photons). Why correct: Higher kVp → higher energy photons → greater penetrating power. Used to adjust contrast vs penetration. Why A wrong: mA (tube current) controls quantity — number of electrons → number of X-ray photons produced (dose). Why C wrong: Exposure time × mA = mAs → controls total quantity/dose. Why D wrong: Filtration (aluminum) removes low-energy photons (hardens beam) but kVp is the primary controller. Clinical relevance: Increasing kVp → lower contrast (more uniform penetration). Decreasing kVp → higher contrast (useful for mammography). Exam trap: kVp = Quality/Penetration; mA = Quantity/Dose. This is the FUNDAMENTAL distinction. Memory aid: 'kVp = Quality (kilovoltage = power); mA = Amount (milliampere = dose).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Physics.

questions.optionB: Absorbed dose (energy deposited per unit mass of tissue — 1 Gy = 1 J/kg)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Gray = absorbed dose; Sievert = effective dose; Becquerel = activity. Why correct: Gray (Gy): absorbed dose = energy deposited per unit mass (1 Gy = 1 J/kg). Replaces old unit 'rad' (1 Gy = 100 rad). Why A wrong: Activity = Becquerel (Bq) = disintegrations per second. Old unit: Curie. Why C wrong: Effective dose = Sievert (Sv) = absorbed dose × radiation weighting factor × tissue weighting factor. Accounts for biological effect. Why D wrong: Exposure = Coulombs/kg (or Roentgen — old unit). Only for X/gamma in air. Clinical relevance: CT abdomen ≈ 10 mSv, CXR ≈ 0.02 mSv. Deterministic effects have dose thresholds; stochastic (cancer) have no threshold. Exam trap: Gray = physical dose absorbed; Sievert = biological equivalent dose. Don't mix them up. Memory aid: 'Gray = Given energy; Sievert = biological Significance.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Physics.
questions.conceptTag: Gray absorbed dose Sievert effective Becquerel

questions.questionText: ALARA principle in radiation protection stands for:
questions.optionB: As Low As Reasonably Achievable (optimizing radiation protection — minimize dose while obtaining diagnostic information)
questions.explanation: Core concept: ALARA is the fundamental principle of radiation protection. Why correct: Optimize exposure to be As Low As Reasonably Achievable while maintaining diagnostic quality. Three pillars of radiation protection: Time (minimize), Distance (increase — inverse square law), Shielding (lead barriers). Why others wrong: Made-up acronym expansions. Clinical relevance: Applies to all medical radiation: avoid unnecessary scans, use lowest dose protocols, shield radiosensitive organs, use alternatives (USG/MRI) when possible. ICRP dose limits: 20 mSv/year for workers, 1 mSv/year for public. Exam trap: ALARA does NOT mean 'no radiation' — it means optimizing the dose-benefit ratio. Pregnancy: fetal dose <100mGy considered safe. Memory aid: 'ALARA = Always Limit, Always Reasonably Achieve safety.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Physics.
questions.conceptTag: ALARA radiation protection time distance shielding

questions.questionText: Inverse square law in radiation states that intensity of radiation:
questions.optionB: Is inversely proportional to the SQUARE of the distance from the source (doubling distance → 1/4 intensity)
questions.explanation: Core concept: I ∝ 1/d² — intensity decreases with square of distance. Why correct: Doubling the distance from a radiation source reduces intensity to 1/4. Tripling → 1/9. This is the basis for DISTANCE as a radiation protection measure. Why A wrong: Intensity DECREASES, not increases. Why C wrong: Inverse, not direct. Why D wrong: Distance is the most effective protection measure. Clinical relevance: Standing 2 meters away from a patient instead of 1 meter reduces scatter radiation by 75%. Staff should maximize distance during fluoroscopy. Exam trap: Know the math: 2× distance = 1/4 dose; 3× distance = 1/9 dose. Commonly asked as calculation. Memory aid: 'Inverse Square = If you Square the distance, Inversely reduce the dose.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Physics.
questions.conceptTag: Inverse square law distance radiation protection

questions.questionText: The most radiosensitive cells in the body are:
questions.optionB: Lymphocytes (Bergonié-Tribondeau law — cells with high mitotic rate, long mitotic future, and low differentiation are most radiosensitive)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Lymphocytes are the most radiosensitive cells (undergo apoptosis at very low doses). Why correct: Bergonié-Tribondeau law: radiosensitivity ∝ mitotic rate, mitotic future; ∝ 1/differentiation. Though lymphocytes are non-dividing, they are uniquely radiosensitive (undergo interphase death/apoptosis). Hierarchy: lymphocytes > spermatogonia > erythroblasts > intestinal crypt cells > ... > nerve/muscle cells (most radioresistant). Why A wrong: Muscle has low mitotic rate → radioresistant. Why C wrong: Nerve cells are the MOST radioresistant (highly differentiated, non-dividing). Why D wrong: Cartilage is relatively radioresistant. Clinical relevance: Acute radiation syndrome: hematopoietic > GI > CNS (order of sensitivity). Lymphocyte count used as biological dosimeter. Exam trap: Lymphocytes are an EXCEPTION to Bergonié-Tribondeau law — they don't divide but are very sensitive (p53-mediated apoptosis). Memory aid: 'Lymphocytes are Lethally sensitive to radiation.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Physics.
